title: Te Whakamahi Hangarau mō te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Technology for the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will explore how technology can support the restoration and protection of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream flowing through our rohe that holds deep cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The awa is a traditional food gathering site and features in local pūrākau about the taniwha Mangakōtukutuku who shaped the landscape.
learningObjectives: Design and prototype a technological solution that addresses a specific environmental challenge facing Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, such as water quality monitoring or riparian planting support.
Analyze how mātauranga Māori principles (e.g., kaitiakitanga, whakapapa) can be integrated into the design and implementation of technological practices for environmental management.
Collaborate effectively with peers and local kaitiaki to gather data, test ideas, and refine technological solutions based on community feedback and cultural values.

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa
activities.description: Visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua or kaitiaki to hear pūrākau, learn about its cultural significance, and identify current environmental issues (e.g., sedimentation, pollution). Students document observations through sketches, photos, and water testing using basic kits.

activities.title: He Kōrero mō te Hangarau
activities.description: Research existing technological solutions for stream monitoring and restoration (e.g., IoT sensors, drone mapping, digital storytelling apps). Compare these with traditional Māori environmental management practices discussed with knowledge holders.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Hangarau
activities.description: In small groups, students brainstorm and design a prototype technological solution addressing a specific issue identified at the awa. Designs must incorporate both technical functionality (e.g., data collection, user interface) and cultural considerations (e.g., respect for tapu, accessibility for community use).

activities.title: Te Hanga me te Whakamātautau
activities.description: Using available tools (e.g., Micro:bits, sensors, 3D printers, app development platforms), students build and test a working prototype of their solution. They develop a testing plan that includes both technical performance and cultural appropriateness, seeking feedback from peers and kaitiaki.

activities.title: Te Whakaatu me te Whakawhitiwhiti Kōrero
activities.description: Groups present their prototypes and findings to the class and invited community members. They explain how their design reflects kaitiakitanga, addresses the environmental issue, and could be implemented or improved further with community partnership.

resources: Local kaitiaki from Ngāti Kahungunu environmental groups (e.g., representatives from Manaaki Te Awa project)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's environmental monitoring data and tools (e.g., Land Water Aotearoa maps)
Micro:bit kits with water quality sensors and GPS modules for prototyping
Digital copies of 'Ngā Pūrākau o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku' provided by college kaumātua

tasks: Design portfolio including research notes, initial sketches, and rationale for cultural and technical choices
Working prototype of a technological solution for the awa
Presentation to peers and community explaining the solution and its alignment with kaitiakitanga

nzcAlignment: Technological Practice: Planning for practice, brief development, outcome development and evaluation
Nature of Technology: Characteristics of technology, characteristics of technological outcomes
Technology and Society: Current and historical events and trends in technology, influences of technology on society
